quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chi-square test for homogeneity"?

options.text: When the effects of two variables on the response cannot be distinguished from each other because they are intertwined
μ = 1/p; the expected number of trials needed to achieve the first success
Tests H₀: the distribution of a categorical variable is the same across two or more populations
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chi-square test for homogeneity" is defined as: Tests H₀: the distribution of a categorical variable is the same across two or more populations. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Homogeneity vs. independence"?

options.text: The probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value, assuming H₀ is true
V = √(χ²/(n·min(r-1, c-1))); ranges from 0 to 1, measuring the strength of association between two categorical variables
A smooth curve that models the overall shape of a distribution; the total area under the curve equals 1 and represents probability
Same χ² calculation, but homogeneity compares distributions across known groups; independence tests association within one population

quiz_questions.explanation: "Homogeneity vs. independence" is defined as: Same χ² calculation, but homogeneity compares distributions across known groups; independence tests association within one population. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Study design difference"?

options.text: Independence: one random sample classified by two variables; Homogeneity: separate random samples from each population
σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates
Compare R² values, residual plots, and prediction accuracy across candidate models to choose the best-fitting transformation
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: "Study design difference" is defined as: Independence: one random sample classified by two variables; Homogeneity: separate random samples from each population. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Follow-up analysis"?

options.text: (O - E)²/E for each cell; identifies which observed-expected discrepancies drive the overall test result
The variable suspected of influencing or predicting the response variable in a study
A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events
After rejecting H₀, compare individual cell contributions to χ² or construct pairwise comparisons to identify which groups differ

quiz_questions.explanation: "Follow-up analysis" is defined as: After rejecting H₀, compare individual cell contributions to χ² or construct pairwise comparisons to identify which groups differ. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity, which best defines "Expected counts"?

options.text: Calculated the same way as the independence test: E = (row total × column total) / grand total
A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable
t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator
A false positive incorrectly identifies a condition as present; a false negative incorrectly identifies it as absent

quiz_questions.explanation: In Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity, "Expected counts" refers to: Calculated the same way as the independence test: E = (row total × column total) / grand total.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Tests H₀: the distribution of a categorical variable is the same across two or more populations"?

options.text: LINE conditions
Chi-square test for homogeneity
Retrospective vs. prospective study
Percentile

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chi-square test for homogeneity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Same χ² calculation, but homogeneity compares distributions across known groups; independence tests association within one population"?

options.text: Mean (x̄)
Replication
Homogeneity vs. independence
Pooled proportion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Homogeneity vs. independence".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Independence: one random sample classified by two variables; Homogeneity: separate random samples from each population"?

options.text: Explanatory variable
Percentile
Multiplication rule for independents
Study design difference

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Study design difference".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "After rejecting H₀, compare individual cell contributions to χ² or construct pairwise comparisons to identify which groups differ"?

options.text: Follow-up analysis
Median
Coefficient of variation
Percentile

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Follow-up analysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity, which term matches: "Calculated the same way as the independence test: E = (row total × column total) / grand total"?

options.text: Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Coefficient of determination (r²)
Population regression model
Expected counts

quiz_questions.explanation: In Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity, this refers to "Expected counts".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity is accurate?

options.text: Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.
Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.
Common transformations include log(y) vs. x for exponential relationships and log(y) vs. log(x) for power relationships, with back-transformation needed to interpret predictions in original units.
Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity compares the distribution of a categorical variable across two or more independent populations or groups.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: The calculation is identical to the independence test: χ² = Σ(O-E)²/E with df = (r-1)(c-1) and E = (row total × column total)/grand total.
Hypotheses and P-Values form the logical framework for statistical significance testing.
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.

The three principles of experimental design are control (using comparison groups), randomization (assigning treatments randomly to reduce bias), and replication (using enough subjects to detect real effects).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).
Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.
The distinction is in the study design: independence tests sample from one population and classify by two variables, while homogeneity tests sample separately from multiple populations.
Tree diagrams and two-way tables provide systematic visual methods for computing conditional probabilities in complex scenarios.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity compares the distribution of a categorical variable across two or more independent populations or groups.
Controlling Variables involves design strategies to isolate the effect of the treatment variable by holding other variables constant, matching subjects, or using statistical adjustments.
Comparing proportions is essential in clinical trials, A/B testing, and any study comparing success rates between groups.
Residuals and Least-Squares Regression fit a linear model ŷ = a + bx to bivariate data by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (Σ(y - ŷ)²).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: The geometric distribution counts the number of trials until the first success: P(X=k) = (1-p)^(k-1)·p, with mean 1/p.
The p-value measures the probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than the sample result, assuming H₀ is true.
The calculation is identical to the independence test: χ² = Σ(O-E)²/E with df = (r-1)(c-1) and E = (row total × column total)/grand total.
Significance Tests for Slopes test H₀: β₁ = 0 (no linear relationship) using t = b₁/SE(b₁) with df = n-2.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: Comparing the distribution of car color preferences across three countries to determine whether consumer preferences differ internationally
Public health research testing whether smoking status (smoker/non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (yes/no) in a random sample of adults
Environmental scientists using residual plots to verify that a linear model is appropriate for predicting water quality from upstream land use data
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: Analyzing the relationship between study hours and exam scores to determine what proportion (r²) of score variation is explained by study time
Medical trials comparing average recovery times between a new surgical technique and the standard procedure across two independent patient groups
Social media analysis estimating the proportion of posts containing misinformation and computing a confidence interval using the normal approximation to p̂
Clinical trials comparing the distribution of side effects (none, mild, moderate, severe) across three different drug dosages

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Reading Minitab/R/Excel regression output to identify the slope estimate, standard error, and confidence interval for a research report
Environmental scientists using residual plots to verify that a linear model is appropriate for predicting water quality from upstream land use data
Education policy comparing the distribution of reading proficiency levels (below, at, above grade level) across different school districts
Quality control calculating the probability of finding 0, 1, 2, or more defective items in a batch of 50 using the binomial distribution with known defect rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity?

options.text: Medical trials comparing average recovery times between a new surgical technique and the standard procedure across two independent patient groups
Using boxplots and five-number summaries to compare test score distributions across multiple schools or demographic groups
Comparing the distribution of car color preferences across three countries to determine whether consumer preferences differ internationally
Reading Minitab/R/Excel regression output to identify the slope estimate, standard error, and confidence interval for a research report

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity in practice?

options.text: Sociological research examining whether political party affiliation is independent of geographic region using survey data from a national random sample
Market research testing whether product preference is associated with age group to determine whether different demographics need different marketing strategies
Clinical trials comparing the distribution of side effects (none, mild, moderate, severe) across three different drug dosages
FDA drug approval requiring p < 0.05 evidence that a new medication performs better than placebo before allowing it on the market

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chi-Square Test for Homogeneity.
